title: Assembly Theory part 3: A single causal factor explains assembly hierarchies: Intelligent Design.
author: Truman, Royal
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
In principle, complex objects existing in high copy numbers could arise through three mechanisms: (1) natural material causes, (2) intelligent design, and (3) biological processes. Intelligent design and biological processes share critical features, notably the utilization of hierarchical assemblies constructed from a limited set of elementary components. This hierarchical approach confers significant advantages across manufacturing, utilization, maintenance, and recycling phases. In contrast, natural material causes—driven by thermodynamics, kinetics, and reaction mechanisms—lack the capacity for such structured assembly, resulting in an inability to generate comparable complexity or organization.  
  Empirical evidence challenges the notion that essential biomolecules, including proteins, RNA, and DNA, emerged from natural prebiotic assembly pathways, as these processes are insufficiently specific or complex to account for such molecular intricacy. Consequently, contemporary origin-of-life research depends on intelligently designed tools and methodologies to synthesize molecules relevant to life, underscoring the limitations of purely natural mechanisms in creating biological complexity.  
  Moreover, biological technologies demonstrate remarkable sophistication, far exceeding current human technological capabilities. These systems exhibit self-assembly of intricate manufacturing hierarchies, component metabolism, growth, stimulus responsiveness, self-repair, homeostasis, environmental adaptability, extensive information processing, and reproduction. Within cells, dynamic adaptation to shifting demands is achieved by adjusting subassembly copy numbers, highlighting the efficiency and flexibility of biological systems.  
  Assembly Theory quantifies the complexity of objects by determining the minimum number of steps required for their production, revealing striking similarities between intelligently designed technologies and biological processes that are absent in natural abiotic chemistry.
